mcqs.question: Which of the following is the fundamental prerequisite for applying binary search on an array?

options.text: The array must be sorted in ascending order
The array must contain only unique elements
The array must be stored in a linked list
The array must have a size that is a power of 2

mcqs.explanation: Binary search requires a sorted array to work correctly, as it relies on comparing the middle element with the target to eliminate half of the search space.

mcqs.question: In a binary search, what is the maximum number of iterations needed to find an element (or determine its absence) in a sorted array of 64 elements?
mcqs.explanation: Binary search has O(log₂n) time complexity. For n=64, log₂64 = 6, so maximum 6 iterations are needed.

options.text: O(log n)
O(n)
O(n log n)

mcqs.explanation: The iterative implementation uses only three variables (low, high, mid) regardless of input size, giving O(1) space complexity.

mcqs.question: A developer implements binary search but mistakenly uses 'low = mid' instead of 'low = mid + 1' when the target is greater than the middle element. What is the most likely consequence of this error?

options.text: The algorithm will run faster
The algorithm may enter an infinite loop
The algorithm will always return incorrect results
The algorithm will use less memory

mcqs.explanation: Using 'low = mid' when mid is not equal to target can cause low to remain unchanged, potentially leading to an infinite loop if the search space doesn't shrink.

mcqs.question: Given a sorted array with duplicate values [2, 4, 4, 4, 7, 9] and target value 4, what does the standard binary search return?

options.text: The index of the first occurrence (index 1)
The index of the last occurrence (index 3)
The index of any occurrence (could be 1, 2, or 3)
The count of occurrences (3)

mcqs.explanation: Standard binary search returns when it finds any matching element; the specific index returned depends on the implementation but will be one of the positions where 4 exists.

mcqs.question: Binary search can be used to find the insertion position for a target value in a sorted array. For array [1, 3, 5, 7] and target 4, what position should be returned?
mcqs.explanation: The insertion position is where 4 should be placed to maintain sorted order, which is between 3 (index 1) and 5 (index 2), so position 2.

mcqs.question: To find the first occurrence of a target value in a sorted array with duplicates using binary search, which modification is necessary?

options.text: Search only in the left half after finding a match
Continue searching in the left half even after finding a match, storing the index
Use a different data structure entirely
Perform a linear search after finding any match

mcqs.explanation: To find the first occurrence, after finding a match we continue searching the left half and keep updating the result index, ensuring we find the leftmost position.

mcqs.question: In the worst-case scenario, how many comparisons does binary search make when searching in an array of 1000 elements?
mcqs.explanation: Worst-case comparisons equal ceil(log₂1000) = 10, as each comparison eliminates half the remaining elements until only one remains.
